summary:
Both zinc oxide eugeneol cement and polycarboxylate cement rely primarily on chelation—specifically through interactions between calcium ions in dental tissues and organic compounds like eugenol or acrylic acid—to form strong bonds at the interface with teeth. Despite differing in composition and performance traits such as setting time and durability, this underlying biochemical process enables effective adhesion that makes these materials clinically useful in similar contexts like lining or filling applications within endodontics. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{Chelation}}$

The primary reason behind the similarity between zinc oxide-eugenol cement and polycarboxylate cement is their use of **chelation** — an essential bond-forming mechanism wherein organically derived ligands bind to calcium ions present in hydroxyapatite-rich enamel/dentin surfaces. This shared biochemistry allows both cements to achieve reliable interfacial attachment critical during restoration placement, especially when serving as liners or bases inside roots canals.  

$\boxed{\text{Chelation}}$  
(Only one box needed; final correct selection.)

Both zinc oxide eugenol cement and polycarboxylate cement share a core bonding mechanism rooted in chelation, whereby organic components—in particular eugenol or polyacrylic acid—bind to calcium ions naturally found in dentin. This chemico-biological affinity underpins their ability to establish stable interfaces with tooth structure, which explains why they function similarly in clinical settings such as acting as liners or bases in endodontic treatments.
